title: Poker Fundamentals: Opening Ranges and Position

questions.prompt: Which position at a 9-handed table acts last on every postflop street?

answers.explanation: UTG is the first player to act preflop and is in an early postflop position, not last.
The small blind acts first on every postflop street, not last.
The button acts last on the flop, turn, and river, which is the most profitable position at the table.
The big blind acts second-to-last postflop when the button is in the pot, not last.

questions.prompt: From early position, which of these starting hands is generally considered the strongest opening raise?

answers.explanation: AKs is a premium hand that plays well from any position, with strong equity against most opening ranges.
66 is a marginal opener from early position; it set-mines well but loses value out of position.
KTo is dominated by most hands that call an early-position raise and should usually be folded UTG.
J9s is a speculative hand best played in late position, not from early position.

questions.prompt: Effective stacks 100bb. You are on the button with A♠ K♠. UTG opens to 3bb, MP calls, and action folds to you. What is the most theoretically sound action?

answers.explanation: Folding AKs to a single raise and a flat is far too tight; you have one of the strongest hands in the game.

answers.text: Flat call to keep weaker hands in
answers.explanation: Flatting lets the blinds squeeze and gives up the equity edge of three-betting in position with a premium hand.

answers.text: Three-bet to roughly 12bb for value and isolation
answers.explanation: Three-betting AKs in position builds the pot with a hand that flops well, isolates the original raiser, and denies equity to the blinds.

answers.text: Limp behind to disguise hand strength
answers.explanation: Limping behind with AKs is a clear leak; it caps your range and forfeits the initiative.
